sentences.text: Yeah.
Well, the train was going across Slovakia.
It went uphill for quite a long time.
We couldn't see any-- It was dark.
We couldn't see stations, but we knew it was going uphill, so we were going towards the Polish-Hungarian border.
Finally, we reached the highest point, and then from then on the train went downhill.
And early in the morning, we arrived to the first Polish railway station.
We didn't-- don't know what station, but it was already in Poland.
The train went all the time down.
At the railway station, a very interesting thing happened.
I-- I-- I looked out on the window, and I could see about half a dozen German Wehrmacht officers-- not SS, Wehrmacht.
So I-- I was brave enough to-- to-- to speak to them in my German, that I asked them-- I say it in English-- excuse me, officer, I said.
Is it true that all the Jewish people on this train will be executed when we arrive to a-- to a concentration camp?
And he replied.
[GERMAN], he said.
[SPEAKING GERMAN] And everybody who-- it means that you're going to work.
Nobody going to be executed.
You are going to work.
You are going to work hard for Hitler and for the final victory.
And everybody who works hard will get plenty of food.
That's what he said.
That was probably-- that's what he was told.
He probably didn't know it himself.
So it-- everybody was happy.
Oh, we all sure we are going to work hard as long as we get something to eat.

So everybody was in a good, cheerful mood-- apart from the fact that we had already five or six people who were dead, the elderly people, I mean, the-- [? all the-- ?] I mean, they-- they couldn't take the heat, they couldn't take to be without food for-- for three, four days, and they died.
A lot of people were unconscious.
I mean, the older people suffered terribly.
But 20, 21 year old, we thought it's a great adventure, you know?
See what will happen next.
And then we still had some bread in the rucksacks, so we could eat something.
Not much, but we had some bread, and even couple of bites of sausage.
And then the next day, in the morning, then we-- the train stopped, and we looked out and just realized it was a strange town.
But in my background was a huge sign saying, Auschwitz.
I never heard about the word 'Auschwitz.' Nobody ever told us that such a place exists.
We didn't know what it is.
It's Auschwitz.
It's some Polish or German town.
And then all the doors swung open, and [LAUGHS] then all hell broke loose in one second.
Here there they were, about two dozen SS men with their savage dogs-- all the dogs barking at once, jumping, trying to jump at us.
And then the-- the kapos, the-- in the striped uniforms, who were-- some of them were Jewish, most of them were not Jewish-- German criminals who were-- who were also prisoners.
They jumped at us.
[SPEAKING GERAMN] Out, out!
Everybody out!
Leave the luggage.
Leave everything behind.
No need to bring the suitcases or luggage.
That will be transported later.
Leave everything there.
You get it later.
So everybody jumped.
And you couldn't jump, they just pulled people out and throw them down.
[SPEAKING GERMAN] Everybody five, five in a row.
Five in a row.
Men on one side, women on the other side.
[SPEAKING GERMAN]
They didn't give us time to even think, because to avoid the revolts or anything like that, they have to hitting people all the time.
So you-- you-- you couldn't think.
You couldn't form an idea in your head, because you had to dodge the-- the hitting, the beating-- beating your back, your shoulder, your neck, on the face, and whatever.
Quickly, quick, just to avoid the kicking.
So do what you are told.
You know.
So we went up in fives.
And we stuck together, the 200 boys from the brigade.
We jumped up from the three wagons, and the other civilians, well, they formed wherever they could.
And then we had to go up to the four officers.
I'm told that one of them could have been Mengele, but he didn't introduce himself to any of us.
So they were standing there in their polished black boots.

And we could already see what the hell is going on, because the old people and the little children, they all went on one side with all the grandmothers and so forth, and the healthy young ones, they went on the other side.
So we stood in our-- in our rows of fives.
We stuck together, the 200 of us.
And-- and the best-speaking man, the commander of our group, who was a managing director of one of the biggest Hungarian hotels-- a Jewish man, he was about 36-- he, of course he died.
He didn't survive it.
But anyhow, he went up to the SS highest-ranking officer.
He said-- and he could speak beautiful German-- he said, hereby, I report for duty.
This is a special hard-labor brigade, and we wish to advise [? that we're ?] undertaking any hard works.
That's to try to impress the SS, that he can get some good work out from us.
So SS [? said, ?] where are you?
Where is your brigade starting?
Right here, and it goes right down rows of five.
So you know.
[INAUDIBLE].
We had to go at 40 rows of fives.
And if you looked at us, you could see that they are all young people.
There were some 30, 35-year-old, but most of them were 20 to 22-year-olds.
So OK, you, off to the side.
So we-- we were all right.
We went to the right side.
Then other long queues were going-- I could just see what's going on.
Elderly people couldn't walk.
They were very sick.
Just, oh, he's struggling.
Oh, we'll send-- send a-- send the bus for them.
The truck and buses and trucks came up.
And just go up there.
We'll-- we'll-- you'll be all right.
We'll look after you.
And then they all drove up to the--
The other feature of the place was the terrific amount of burnt flesh coming down from the sky.
That-- that was-- you couldn't-- you couldn't get away from it because such a strong burning flesh.
Where is all this burning flesh coming from?
We thought there might be a slaughterhouse nearby or something.
And then we could see the five chimneys in the distance, about maybe half a kilometer away.
And then we started our march into Birkenau.
Birkenau is called also Auschwitz zwei, Auschwitz number two.
But it's called Birkenau as well.
And then we walked.
First we walked past-- we were like in military formation.
We'd quick march.
We moved-- we walked past the other columns, who went very slowly with the women and little children and, you know, the old men.
But we walked twice as fast, so we went past them.
We could see them all marching slowly.
They had no idea where they are matching.
But we went past some women's barracks.
Well, we didn't know they are women.
They looked like creatures from another planet, because they were-- they were all in striped uniforms.
Their head was shaven, of course.
You know, very, very skinny, very skeleton-like figures, like from another planet.
They were-- they were there in a closed, you know, barbed-wire fences.
Behind the fences, they were there.
And then they rushed to the fences when they saw us, and they started to shout in Yiddish and in Hungarian.
They found out where are you from, and I told them Hungary.
So then the Hungarian girls came to the fences.
And they looked horrible.
I can remember them.
They were covered in mud everywhere.
There was mud.
Everywhere was mud, and they were all barefoot or in-- in wooden clogs.
Anyhow, they shouted, throw all the food you have over the fence to us, because you're going to a shower, your disinfection.
They will take everything that you have on you.
Everything.
All the clothing, underpants, or what have you.
You will get nothing back, so throw it over.
Anybody has a pocket knife, please throw it over.
Or a spoon, or a toothbrush.
Toothbrush had big value.
If you have any toothbrush, throw it over to us.
And some people didn't believe them.
They didn't want-- didn't want to part with it.
Some people thrown some things over-- some food, some bread pieces or things like that.
And then they're-- each time we thrown in a piece of bread, about 20 girls jumped on it in the dust, and they were fighting for it for 10 minutes.
So eventually the-- the bread become mixed up in the soil, and they just try to eat a little bit of it.
Fighting destroyed the whole bread.
You know.
Anyhow, eventually we got to our-- our destination, to our barrack, which was a standard Birkenau barrack, of course.
It was built to hold about maybe 150 people.
They put a thousand people in it, very tightly.
And oh yeah, and so the usual procedure-- the-- the barrack commander had a very hard job, because he had to keep order over a thousand people.
And he was just himself and maybe had three or four attendants.
So he had to hit people all the time with a heavy stick to-- to have some discipline, because I mean, a thousand people could easily have overwhelmed him.
He was a fairly well-built German criminal.
He had green triangle, which means he was a criminal.
He was probably a murderer or something like that.
A rapist, maybe.
Anyhow, he kept hitting people.

And he said anybody who has any jewelry on him or hidden away, or built into his shoes, into heels-- because that was a good place to hide diamonds-- hand-- hand it to me right now, because after one hour, we shall make a [INAUDIBLE] check of all shoes and everything.
If I find any jewelry on you, you're gonna be hanged up on that post.
So hanging up there.
Lot of people hang up-- hung up there yesterday.
So you want to join them if you don't give me your gold.
But we didn't give the gold.
I-- I had gold, for instance.
I had the buttons of my uniform.
You know, the buttons were made from gold coins.
My mother arranged it.
They were-- they were black buttons with textile over it.
But I had about 12 of these gold coins, which, of course, was all left in the-- in the disinfection room.
Anyhow, so-- the conditions were terrible in this place.
We had no bread.
For 24 hours, we had nothing.
After 24 hours, everybody got a little piece of margarine, about 50 grams, which some people couldn't eat on its own.
You know, you're hungry, you had no food for four days, and then they give you a piece of margarine.
But I tried to eat it.
I managed to get some of it down.
What happened is that the commander of the block, he got the bread portion all right, but he kept it for himself.
He-- he could sell it for some onions and other things.
So he didn't give us the bread.
He just gave us the margarine.
Well, the day after that, we got a slice of bread.
Then we got a soup, which was so horrible that a lot of people just refused to eat it.
But I said, eat it, because it doesn't matter.
Like a medicine, eat it.
Just eat it.
It had some-- maybe it had some beetroot in it, as well a lot of other stuff-- some potato peel.
But it was a terrible soup.
[LAUGHS] So I tried to eat it with great difficulty with the bread, so-- just to have some nourishment.
But lot of people said I-- I rather throw it out.
I won't-- I won't touch it.
So conditions were very bad.
Then we got our tattoos next morning.
Everybody had burnt in their number.
We started with capital A 12,000 like-- oh, my number was 12139.
And we found that capital A was necessary because previously 200,000 people had their tattoos, and as they got to the number of 200,000, then the Germans said let's start again with 0.
And then they put a A-series.
The A-series went from 0 up to 20,000, and then by July the B-- the B-series started.
We met the B people later on.
B always meant that they only arrived in July '44, July-August.
The A-series, they-- they all arrived in 1944.
Anybody who had a number between 100,000 and 200,000 was 1943, and anybody with a very small number must have been there since 1941-- with a number of, say, 27,000.
Oh, that was very, very, very few people.
Each time you met somebody with a number under 100,000, you-- you really had the highest regard for him, because he survived there three or four years.
So he had-- must have had a good position or some extremely heavy physique.
Anyhow, they got our numbers.
The sleeping conditions were shocking.
It was four people in each bunk, four-level bunk.
It was-- just to climb up to the top was hard enough, and then four legs came into your mouth and the other four legs went into the other people's face, of course.
And kicking, shouting.
It's people kicking each other all night.
And each time there was a noise, the commander came there with his stick and kept shut up, you bastards!
All of you make so much noise.
You know, they made noise because people were kicking each other in-- in their sleep.
So he-- he had a hard job to keep 1,000 people-- controlling 1,000 people.
Anyhow, then-- then he gave us the big secret that the only way, how do we get out from this?
The only way we out from here is through the chimney.
We say, what do you mean?
Well, people, when they get weaker and, you know, after work too much after three, four months, you get run down.
Then they put you into the gas chamber like you-- like we did yesterday with all your women and children.
And then you go up the chimney.
After they gas you, then they-- you get to the-- then they burn you.
And we didn't quite believe it, but eventually we believed it.
Eventually, after a few weeks, we believed it.
And then he said, you are very lucky.
Now that's an important point.
He said, you are very lucky because you got your numbers.
That means that you are going to be a regular worker.
You are going to be sent away from Auschwitz II, from Birkenau.
You are going to go over to Auschwitz II, and from there you are going to different workplaces.
You're going to do work which is important for Hitler and the Reich.
So you have a chance to survive, possibly.
But us, me, he became friendly at this stage, after three, four days.
We who are here in Birkenau, we are all going to be gassed.
Every one of us.
Because Birkenau is just like it a reserve stock for the Germans.
We have here people ranging from 50,000 to 100,000, and each time more transports come in, then they gas as many as-- as the crematorium can burn.
And their-- additional, the surplus stocks, go to Birkenau, where they're kept in reserve.
And then there is the day when there's a no-- no train coming, which happened after the Hungarian Jews were all fixed up, liquidated.
And then when there were-- a week when there were no trains coming, then on that week, they pull out every day 10,000, 20,000 people from Birkenau, and then they gas them, using up their reserves.
So to say, they-- they reserve raw material-- like reserve for the fire, for the-- for the-- for the crematorium.
That was their reserve.
The only exception was, which they didn't use up at that stage, was the gypsy camp, which was only used up around June or July.
And then there was the Theresienstadt camp where who could keep their civilian clothing.
They were eventually burnt.
They were-- they were gassed, but not yet.
They were gassed later.
I believe they were gassed around November.
It was October or November.
So they were not gassed in June, July.
They were kept.
They didn't know what to do with them, quite, but eventually they decided that they should be gassed too.
But in the meantime, this fellow was very sad, because he said, you're going to Auschwitz, but I'm here.
And could be a quiet day next week or in the fortnight, when I will be gassed.
And they were.
And we saw many, many groups of people driven a savage way to the-- towards the gas chamber next day, in a different-- not our block, but next door where the others were.
Another feature of the place was the heavy rain, the colossal mud.
Each time we had to go to the toilet in groups of 10, we had to go through really deep mud, which was quite a job.
And it was raining, and the rain came through the roof.
And it was very cold at night.
No food, shivering, cold.
Everybody coughing, sneezing.
So that was the main features of this Birkenau.
But after a few days, we-- as he said, he revealed-- we had to march.
And we went through.
We marched for about one hour.
And we marched from Auschwitz II to Auschwitz I.
And then we arrived to the main Auschwitz camp, to the 28 big brick buildings, every building holding up to 600, 800 people.
And a special ladies-- women's section.
There-- there were 5,000, 6,000 women usually.
That was the arrival to Auschwitz.
In Auschwitz, well, when we arrived, we were still in reasonably good condition-- very hungry, terribly hungry.
But then on top floor, the-- the older-- older inmates and a couple SS men, they had a good joke.
They-- they tore off a piece of bread and they threw it down to us.
We were standing in a crowd, and a piece of bread come.
Everybody would jump on it, like-- like you would see sparrows jumping on a piece of bread and fighting.
And I-- I didn't.
After ?] [? all, ?] I said, I better not fight for it, because you can get beaten, and, you know, and at the end you don't get any bread anyhow.
So they were laughing.
Look how they fight for the piece of bread!
So anyhow, then-- then we got-- Every day, we got called, put into sections.
Some-- some people went to work in the coal mines.
It was terrible work.
It was horrible.
They came back at night covered in-- And you couldn't wash.
You couldn't change.
You had to sleep in the same uniform.
And other people got called up to factory work, different-- different factories, heavy machinery, the road building.
And I was fairly lucky.
I-- I was put into a [SPEAKING GERMAN], which is potato, deliveries, and emptying of the wagons, the trains which came in with any type of goods.
And the goods sometimes included cement-- cement bricks, or bricks, or sand, or sometime potatoes, and sometimes onions.
So then-- then we got-- then we got-- well, I think the usual Auschwitz, I mean-- So many books were written about it.
I don't know.
You think I should go on about Auschwitz?
No?
Yeah?
Well, we had to get up early in the morning, about 4 o'clock.
Then we had to stand one or two hours outside in the cold, wait for the SS men to count if the numbers are right.
There was one missing, then we had to count other hour.
Check every room, everything.
If they's gone missing, then we couldn't do anything.
You couldn't get coffee or bread until everything was right.
And then after we had to march.
We had to march out, run out to our workplace.
We had to-- on the one side was the military band playing German Army march music.
On the other side was the central brothel, where about 20 or 30 Jewish girls were waving us goodbye.
And then we went out through the main gate, [SPEAKING GERMAN], with the SS.
If they saw something, somebody not stepping on time, then they came over and gave us a big bash on the face.
That was lucky, because the old-timers said previous years they used to shoot them right away on the spot.
But in my time, by-- by middle of '44, they only bashed them up or kicked them or punched them in the eye or somewhere.

We went to the workplace, and there we had to-- there was a Polish kapo who was beating everybody, because we're not used to-- we-- we had to be broken in to [? hard. ?] We had to take the bags of potatoes-- so the men not walking, but running with it.
We had to run with it.
And we only had wooden clogs.
It was very hard to run in a wooden clog.
You know, Dutch-- only a piece of timber.
And to run in it with a heavy bag on your back, it's very, very difficult.
And if it was not quick, if you dropped it, then-- then he came there and hit you 10 times for it.
So that was the work in Auschwitz.
Well, I could go on for hours.
It's-- at lunchtime, we had half an hour break.
Then we had a soup, which was mostly potato peel and a bit of cabbage in it.
And then in the evening, marched-- we marched back.
And doesn't matter, rain or whatever.
It was luckily summer, so it was only cold in the morning.
A lot of rain.
If you were wet, then you had to sleep in the wet uniform-- no-- no nightgown or pajamas, of course.
We got back.
The worst thing was I remember a couple very bad days.
And we got back trembling from hunger and queued up for our evening soup.
And eventually, I got my thing with a half a liter of soup in it.
And the Russian prisoner of war run up to me, punched me in the nose, and took my soup.
He took away my soup and run away, disappeared in one minute.
There I was after working all day, and I didn't have my soup.
So I went to my friends, I said look, he just robbed me of my soup.
And they each gave me one or two spoonful of their soup, so I had half a-- half the amount of soup.
It was terrible.
Hunger was terrible, in the first-- first weeks, especially.
And then-- then we had luckier weeks, because sometimes we had to unload the wagons.
The new arrivals left behind the rucksacks.
And then they'd unload them, classify them.
We could pinch a bit of vitamin tablets now and then.
And other lucky things, sometimes the onions came in, and we could eat.
The raw onions we could eat.
We tried the raw potatoes.
We couldn't eat the raw potatoes, but the raw onions we could eat.
It'd burn the stomach a bit.
And then sometimes came a big load of cabbage for the soup, for the cabbage soup.
That was our best time, because then we'd just dug into the cabbages.
Whenever the SS men went the other way, we just gobbled up the bit of cabbage.
And so we worked for six or seven weeks like that, when my best friend-- I'll try to speak-- tell the story of this man.
He was my best friend for eight years in the high school.
He was a brilliant scholar, was the best in the class.
Despite being Jewish, he was the best in class.
So he was brilliant.
And he had his clogs, and the clogs running bruised-- bruised his-- his legs terribly.
And the legs got infected with the sand and cement, and they got terribly swollen, full with pus.
So in the evening, he went to the so-called Krankenbau, which is hospital in English.
There were every evening about 600, 800 people standing, trying to get in, and every evening they admitted about 30 or 40.
So 5% got in.
We had to be really terribly sick to get in.
But anyhow, he went up queuing there four times, and the fourth time, they let him in.
They let him in, and he got treatment.
Why I'm telling it is because after a few weeks they selected him-- officers came, and they picked him out and 28 other boys for medical experimentation.
They picked him out and the others.
And then after one week, he came back with all-- all his leg was full with pus.
And they injected all sorts of bacteria in him.
So they had to cut off-- they had to amputate his leg after about two weeks when that happening.
So his leg-- his leg was amputated, but he was still alive.
We knew that.
We went to visit him sometimes.
And then came my turn.
After about seven or eight weeks, very early in August, my leg was also bruised.
Dirt went into it.
Badly swollen, full with pus.
So I-- I just did the same what my friend did.
Went every evening trying to get in, trying to get in.
I don't know, by some miracle, eventually the third or fourth night they let me into the hospital.
I got into the hospital.
Jewish doctors-- I'm just cutting it short-- they treated me, and I was getting better.
I was there in the big-- big room of the hospital.
Straightaway, the hunger was terrible, because being sick and not able to work, they only gave us half of the usual not-so-generous portions.
We only got half of the half-- half of the little slice of bread and only half of the soup.
The hunger was terrible, but we didn't have to work.
We could lie in bed.
In the morning, we had to have a cold wash down and so forth.
And we got treatment.
I got treatment for my-- got some cream on it, or something, and it got much better.
And I was in there when Paris was liberated by-- by the Americans.

We knew it because the SS officer, when he finished with his sausages [LAUGHS] and his [? eierspatzle ?], the egg with the bacon, then he read his paper in disgust, for the Germans were retreating everywhere.
And he throw the paper to us, going ugh, scheisse.
Paris has fallen to the Americans.
So I read this.
I knew that Paris has fallen, and the Americans were still advancing towards the German border.
So I was still inside.
And then I got much better, and the talk was, by the commander of the barrack, that next week you are going back to work.
I was horrified by the fact.
It was already starting to get colder.
It was, you know, end the of August, September.
And I went back to work.
Yeah.
